chunk_summary: Guidelines for managing growth faltering in children, including weight velocity targets, decision timelines, and escalation strategies for inadequate response to nutritional interventions.
learning_objectives: Understand weight velocity targets for different age groups in growth faltering.
Learn decision timelines for reassessing and escalating interventions in growth faltering.
Identify red flags requiring earlier escalation in growth faltering management.
Incorporate evidence-based strategies, such as energy density adjustments and oral nutritional supplements, into care plans.
key_facts: Catch-up growth is defined as weight gain 2–3 times the average rate for age.
Weight velocity targets: 0–3 months: 26–31 g/day; 3–6 months: 17–18 g/day; 6–9 months: 12–13 g/day; 9–12 months: 9 g/day; >12 months: 7–9 g/day.
Track weight gain over 1–2 weeks (g/day or g/kg/day in severe wasting) rather than relying on single measurements.
Two-week decision point: Reassess feeding mechanics, meal structure, and energy displacement if weight velocity is below target.
Four-week decision point: Consider oral nutritional supplements if weight response remains inadequate despite verified adherence.
Escalate earlier for red flags such as edema, weight-for-length/height <-3 SD, persistent vomiting/diarrhea, or systemic illness.
reference_quote: NICE recommends considering oral nutritional supplements when faltering growth continues despite other interventions and reviewing regularly to decide continuation.
document_key_topics: Weight velocity targets for growth faltering
Escalation strategies for inadequate growth response
Red flags in pediatric growth faltering
